A present-tense verb. The a- prefix at the front marks the doer as 'I' (first person singular). The final -u ending is the indicative mood, meaning the action is stated as a plain, ongoing fact ('I worship'), not a command or wish. Coming after lā, it reads 'I do not worship'.
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, serve, submit as a devoted servant
'I worship / I serve' — to direct one's devotion, obedience and lowly submission to a deity. In Qur'anic usage this is the total servanthood owed to Allah alone: prayer, reverence and surrender.

A relative word meaning 'what' or 'that which'. It is the object of the verb before it ('I worship [what]') and at the same time sets up the verb after it ('what you worship'). It is a fixed particle with no case ending to parse.
'what' — a connector standing for the thing(s) the listeners worship. It points to their objects of worship (their idols), introducing the clause 'what you worship'.
you (do) worship / serve (masculine plural) you all

A present-tense verb addressed to a group. The ta- prefix marks the doer as 'you', and the -ūna ending is a single, indivisible suffix that makes the subject masculine plural ('you all') while also marking the indicative mood, stating the action as a fact ('you worship').…
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, serve, submit as a devoted servant
'you (all) worship / you serve' — the act of devotion offered by the disbelievers being addressed, here directed toward their idols and false deities.

verses.en: Nor do you worship what I worship—you have not surrendered to Allah alone as I have been commanded to do.
words: Two particles fused together. 'wa-' is the conjunction 'and', linking this verse to the one before. 'lā' is the negating particle 'not'; placed before a noun sentence here, it denies the statement as a whole. Neither word changes form for case or tense — particles are fixed.

And not — a connecting word ('and') joined to a negating word ('not'). Together they extend the previous verse's denial: 'and likewise not...'. Here the negation covers a whole noun-based sentence, denying in the present that the addressees are worshippers of what the speaker worships.

A standalone subject pronoun, second person masculine plural. The '-tum' part is the marker for 'you all (men)'. As a pronoun it is indeclinable — it never takes case endings; it simply functions here as the subject of the sentence, with 'worshippers' as the predicate.
You — the detached pronoun addressing a group of males directly ('you all'). Being a standalone (not attached) pronoun, it carries emphasis: it spotlights 'you' as the subject the sentence is about.
worshipper / one who worships (masculine plural ending)

An active participle, masculine plural. The '-ūna' ending marks it as plural, masculine, and in the nominative (subject) case — fitting because it is the predicate describing 'you' (antum). The participle form (shape ʿābid) names the doer of the act.…
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, serve, devote oneself

Worshippers — those who serve and devote themselves in worship. This is an active participle (a 'doer' word, like 'worshipping ones'), describing the addressees as people who perform worship, rather than a finite verb pinned to one tense.

A relative pronoun meaning 'what' or 'that which'. It heads a relative clause completed by the verb that follows (mā aʿbudu = 'that which I worship'). It is fixed in form — no case ending. The whole relative clause it introduces, not the word mā by itself, serves as the object of 'worshippers'.

What — a relative word standing for the object of worship ('that which' the speaker worships). Though 'mā' usually points to things, here it refers reverently to the One the speaker worships, Allah; the form is left general to mark the sharp contrast between the two objects of worship.

A present-tense verb, first person singular. The 'a-' prefix means 'I' (the one doing the action). The final '-u' ending marks the indicative mood — a plain, ongoing statement of fact ('I worship', habitually and presently), not a command or a negated/subjunctive form. With 'mā' it forms 'what I worship'.
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, serve, devote oneself
I worship — the speaker affirms his own act of worship, directed to Allah alone. Paired with 'mā' before it, the phrase means 'that which I worship', i.e. the true object of the speaker's devotion.

verses.en: I will never worship what you worship—no social pressure, no compromise for peace at the price of shirk, will change that.
words: Two particles fused into one word. The prefix wa- means "and" and ties this verse to the one before it. lā is the negation "not"; here it negates the noun-based (nominal) sentence that follows ("I am not a worshipper of..."), giving an absolute, ongoing denial rather than a one-time "I did not."

"And not" — a connective "and" joined to the negative word "not." Together they open the statement with "And I am not...," linking this verse to the previous declaration and firmly negating what follows.

An independent subject pronoun ("I"), not attached to any verb. It is indeclinable, so its ending never changes for case. Placed here as the subject (mubtadaʾ) of a nominal sentence ("I [am] a worshipper..."), its presence adds emphasis and contrast: "I, on my side."

"I (am)" — the standalone, emphatic pronoun for the first person singular, here the subject of "I am a worshipper..." Arabic shows "I" in its verb endings, so stating anā separately adds weight, as if to say "I, for my part."
worshipper / one who worships a (indefinite marker)

An active participle (the "-er" form) on the pattern fāʿil, meaning "a worshipper / one worshipping." The final -un is nunation carrying the -u (nominative) vowel: the -u marks it as the predicate (khabar) of the nominal sentence ("I [am] a worshipper"), and the -n shows it is indefinite ("a" worshipper, not "the").…
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, serve, devote oneself
"a worshipper" — one who worships or devotes acts of worship. It is a doer-noun describing the speaker as the kind of person who performs worship, here being denied: "I am not one who worships..."
what / that which (here: the act of)

Indeclinable particle, so it has no case ending of its own. Two readings: (1) the dominant one for this verse, mā masdariyyah (infinitival) — it turns the following verb into a verbal noun, "your worshipping / your mode of worship," yielding "I will never adopt the way you worship";…

"what / that which" — the connector introducing what the verb after it expresses. In this verse the preferred classical reading (Ibn Kathir) takes mā as infinitival (masdariyyah), so it means "the way/act of worshipping" rather than the object worshipped: "...a worshipper of the manner in which you worship." (On the other reading it is the relative "that which," i.e. the thing worshipped — the sense it carries back in verse 2.)
A perfect-tense verb with the attached subject ending -tum, "you," masculine plural — "you (all) worship / have worshipped." The doer is built into the suffix, so there is no separate prefix.…
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, serve, devote oneself

"you worship" — addressed to a group: "the way you worship" (or, on the relative reading, "what you worship"). Although the verb form is grammatically perfect, the context (reflected in the JSON translation "I will never worship what you worship") frames it as the disbelievers' ongoing and continuing practice, not merely a past act.

verses.en: Nor will you ever worship what I worship—until truth enters the heart, no argument can force genuine submission.
words: This is the prefix particle wa- ("and"), used to connect clauses. It carries no case ending of its own; it simply attaches to whatever follows and ties this line to the one before it. In the running verse it pairs with the negative that follows to give "and not / nor."
The conjunction "and," joining this statement to the previous verse so the disavowal continues and accumulates: "and..." It links verse 5 to verse 4, stacking one rejection upon another.

This is the negating particle lā. It is invariable — it never takes a case ending and never changes form. It works by denying the statement that follows; combined with the earlier wa- it yields "and not / nor."

A particle of negation, "not." Here it negates a noun-style (participle) sentence, producing the sense "you are not (worshippers)..." — and with the preceding "and" it reads "nor." Paired with the future-leaning participle it conveys a settled, ongoing refusal: "nor will you ever..."

This is a standalone subject pronoun, second person masculine plural. The piece ant- carries the "you," and the ending -um marks "plural / more than two males." Being an independent pronoun it has a fixed shape and takes no case vowel; placed first, it stresses the subject — "as for you..."

The detached pronoun "you," addressed to a group of men (the disbelievers of Quraysh). Standing on its own before the predicate, it is emphatic — it spotlights the addressees: "you (specifically), you people..."
worshipper / one who worships (masculine plural ending)
This is an active participle, masculine plural. The stem ʿābid means "one who worships"; the ending -ūna marks masculine plural in the subject (nominative) case —…
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, to serve, to devote oneself

"Worshippers / ones who worship" — an active participle describing the addressees as people who perform worship. A participle names the doer rather than a single moment of action, so with the negation it denies that they are, or will be, worshippers of what the Prophet worships; this nuance points to the future: "nor will you ever be worshippers of..."
that which / (my) manner of I worship (indicative-mood ending)

"Mā" is an invariable particle with no case ending; here it is read either as a relative pronoun ("that which") or, in the interpretation the source prefers for this verse, as mā maṣdariyya (an infinitival particle that recasts the following verb as a verbal noun, "my worship / way of worshipping").…
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, to serve, to devote oneself

Two words written together: the particle "mā" plus the verb "I worship." The particle "mā" here can be read two ways. (1) As a relative pronoun ("that which / whom"): "that which I worship" — i.e., the One (Allah) the Prophet devotes himself to, paralleling verse 3. (2) As an infinitival/gerundive particle (mā maṣdariyya), which the source's tafsir (Ibn Kathir) prefers for this verse: it turns the verb into a verbal noun, so the phrase means "my worshipping / my manner of worship." On this reading verse 5 is not a repeat of verse 3: verses 2–3 contrast the differing objects of worship, while verses 4–5 contrast the differing modes of worship — "nor will you adopt the manner in which I worship."

verses.en: For you is your deen—the way of life you have chosen. And for me is mine—the path of tawhid I will not abandon.
words: for / belonging to you (plural, men addressed)

A preposition plus an attached pronoun. The preposition is li- ("for/to"), but when the pronoun -kum attaches, its vowel shifts from -i to -a, giving la-kum. The -kum ending means "you all" (masculine plural), addressing the disbelievers. As a prepositional phrase placed first, it carries the sense "to you belongs...".

"For you" / "yours is" — a phrase that assigns something to the people being addressed (the disbelievers). It opens an equational sentence meaning roughly "belonging to you is...", setting up the idea that they are left with what is theirs.
religion / way of life / requital (subject-marking ending) your (plural, men addressed)

A noun with a possessive ending. The -u vowel on dīn-u is the nominative case: it marks this word as the subject of the verbless sentence ("your religion [is] for you"). The attached -kum means "your" (masculine plural), so together: "your religion."
د-ي-ن (d-y-n) — religion, way of life, judgement/requital

"Your religion" — your whole way of life, your code of belief, worship, and conduct, and the accountability that comes with it. The Arabic word dīn is broader than the English "religion": it covers creed, the path one follows, and the requital one earns for it.

Three pieces joined: the connector wa- ("and"), the preposition li- ("for"), and the pronoun for "me." When the "me" pronoun attaches to li-, it surfaces with an -a vowel here (li-ya), giving wa-li-ya. The phrase means "and to me belongs...".

"And for me" / "and mine is" — the counterpart phrase that assigns something to the speaker (the Prophet, peace be upon him). It mirrors "for you" in the first half, drawing a clean line between the two sides.
religion / way of life / requital (remnant of "my")
The same noun dīn, here ending in -i. In this Qur'anic reading the possessive "my" (the yāʾ) is dropped at the end of the verse, and the leftover kasra (-i) stands in its place —…
د-ي-ن (d-y-n) — religion, way of life, judgement/requital
"My religion" — my own way of life, creed, worship, and the reward it brings; the same rich word dīn as before, now claimed by the speaker. The verse ends by leaving each side with its own path.

surahs.name: النَّصْر
surahs.why: Three verses with إذا، جاء، رأيت، كان — four of the highest-frequency words in the Quran, in their natural habitat.

verses.en: When Allah's decisive help arrives and victory becomes visible—not the small wins of ego, but the opening you could not manufacture yourself—
words: An indeclinable particle, so it has no case ending that changes. It introduces a future event treated as certain to occur and signals that a 'main clause' (the response) is still coming later in the passage.

A time-conjunction meaning 'when' that points to a definite, expected future event — not an uncertain 'if'. It frames the whole verse: it sets up the moment in which something is bound to happen and waits for the response that will follow in verse 3.

A past-tense verb, third-person masculine singular ('he/it came'), matching its subject 'help' which follows. It carries no added person/gender suffix — the bare third-masculine-singular form; the fatḥa on the final letter (the hamza) is part of the verb's own shape, not a separate tense or person marker.…
'Came / has come' — the act of arriving or coming to pass. Though past in form, here it conveys an event so certain it is spoken of as already accomplished.

A masculine noun functioning as the subject of 'came'. The final -u ending (nominative) marks it as the doer of the verb — it is the help that 'comes'. It is also the first part of a possessive pair, joined to 'Allah' next ('the help OF Allah').
Decisive help, aid, or support that brings victory — the kind of backing that turns the tide. Here it is the divine assistance granted by Allah.
Allah (the divine name) of — possessive ending

The divine name in the genitive (possessive) case, shown by the final -i ending, which links it to the preceding word: 'the help OF Allah'. The name اللّٰه is treated by Arabic grammarians as a single, undivided proper noun;…
Allah — the proper name of the one true God. Here in the possessive ('of Allah'), showing that the help just mentioned belongs to and comes from Him alone.
and the victory / opening, conquest (subject marker)

'wa-' is 'and'; 'al-' is 'the'. The noun is a second subject of 'came', sharing the verb with 'help'. Its final -u ending (nominative) marks it as part of what 'comes'. The leading 'al-' makes it definite — THE victory, the well-known promised one.

And the victory — specifically the opening/conquest (of Mecca). The Arabic word carries the sense of an 'opening': a breakthrough that swings doors wide after they were shut, here the triumphant opening of Mecca.

verses.ar: وَرَأَيۡتَ ٱلنَّاسَ يَدۡخُلُونَ فِي دِينِ ٱللَّهِ أَفۡوَاجٗا
verses.en: and you see people entering Allah's deen in waves, no longer mocking what they once dismissed,
words: A past-tense verb. The wa- at the front simply means "and," joining this to the previous verse. The core verb ra'ā means "to see." The final -ta is the subject ending that means "you" (one man) did the action — here, the Prophet himself. So the whole is "and you saw / witnessed."
ر-أ-ي (r-ʾ-y) — to see, perceive, behold (the middle radical is the hamza, ء)

"And you saw / and you see" — addressed directly to the Prophet (peace be upon him). It describes him witnessing with his own eyes the people streaming into Islam. Though grammatically past tense, it points to a coming reality made so certain it is portrayed as already seen.

A noun with "the" attached at the front (the l of al- assimilates into the n, so it sounds like an-nās). The final -a is the accusative ending: it marks this word as the direct object — the thing being seen. So it answers "you saw whom? — the people."
أ-ن-س (ʾ-n-s) — humans, sociability; nās comes from unās, with the initial hamza dropped
"The people / mankind" — humanity at large, the crowds of Arabs who were entering the faith. The definite article "the" makes it the people as a known, collective whole.
they (he-type, marks present tense) enter, go in they (masculine plural)

A present/ongoing-tense verb. The ya- prefix signals the present tense and a third-person subject, and the -ūna ending makes it "they" (many men) and keeps the verb in its normal (indicative) state, i.e. simple ongoing action: "they are entering." It describes the people of the previous word.
"They are entering / going in" — pouring into the religion. The form pictures an ongoing, unfolding action: wave after wave stepping into Islam, not a single finished event.

A preposition (a small linking word) meaning "in" or "into." It has no endings that change; it governs the noun after it, putting that noun into the genitive case. Here it links "entering" to its destination, "the religion of Allah."
— (a particle; it has no triliteral root)
"Into / in" — a preposition expressing entry and immersion: moving into and settling within Allah's religion.
religion, way of life, obedience to Allah

A noun. The final -i ending is genitive for two reasons working together: it follows the preposition fī ("in"), and it links to the next word to mean "the religion OF…" So it reads "into the religion of [Allah]."
"The religion / way of life" — dīn means far more than ritual; it is the whole God-given way: belief, worship, law, obedience and submission to Allah, lived as a complete path.
The proper name Allah. The final -i ending is genitive, showing possession after the preceding word: "the religion OF Allah." It links the religion to its owner.
— (the proper name of Allah; not derived from a triliteral root in the ordinary way)
"Allah" — the proper name of the One True God, the sole object of worship. Here it is the One whose religion the people are entering.
in crowds, in throngs, in troops (groups of people)

A single plural noun in the accusative indefinite (its ending carries tanwīn al-fatḥ, the "-an" you hear). It is not the direct object here; it works as a ḥāl — a word describing the state or manner in which the people entered: "crowd after crowd, in throngs." So: they were entering the religion of Allah in droves.
ف-و-ج (f-w-j) — a group, troop, or band of people
"In crowds / in throngs / wave upon wave" — afwājan is the plural of fawj, a troop or band; the plural pictures group after group, multitudes flooding in together rather than one by one.

verses.ar: فَسَبِّحۡ بِحَمۡدِ رَبِّكَ وَٱسۡتَغۡفِرۡهُۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ تَوَّابَۢا
verses.en: then glorify your Lord with deep praise and seek His forgiveness—for even on the day of triumph, a servant remains in need of mercy, and He is ever the One who accepts tawbah.
words: so / then (in response to what came before) glorify! / declare (Him) free of all imperfection

"fa-" is a connecting prefix meaning "so/then," linking this response to the conditions in verses 1-2. "sabbiḥ" is a form II (doubled-middle-letter) command verb, which makes the action intensive and deliberate: to actively and repeatedly declare Allah's perfection.…
س-ب-ح (s-b-ḥ) — to swim/glide away; hence to declare Allah utterly removed and free from all imperfection (glorification, tasbīḥ)

So glorify and declare the absolute perfection of Allah — proclaim that He is utterly free of any flaw, partner, or deficiency. The opening "so/then" ties this command to the events just described (the help and the victory): when they arrive, respond with this praise.
with / by means of the praise (of)

"bi-" is an attached preposition meaning "with/by." Any noun after it must take the genitive case, which is why "ḥamd" appears with a final "-i" (ḥamdi). That same "-i" ending also chains it to the next word, giving "praise OF (your Lord)."
ح-م-د (ḥ-m-d) — praise; commending someone for their excellence and goodness

with the praise of — i.e., couple your glorification with grateful praise. The phrase joins two acts: declaring Allah free of fault while at the same time affirming the beautiful qualities and blessings for which He is praised.
Lord / Master and Sustainer your (one male)

"rabb" is the first term of a possessive construct ("the Lord OF you" = "your Lord"), and it joins directly to the possessor "-ka" without any separate word for "of." Its final "-i" (rabbi) is the genitive case carried over from the preposition "bi-" two words back, which governs this whole "praise-of-your-Lord" phrase…
ر-ب-ب (r-b-b) — to nurture, master, sustain; one who owns and brings up

your Lord — the One who created you, owns you, sustains and nurtures you, and to whom you are answerable. "Rabb" bundles together the ideas of master, owner, sustainer, and the one who lovingly brings something to its completion; here it is made personal by "your."
and seek forgiveness! / ask for pardon Him

"wa-" means "and." The verb is form X, marked by the "ist-" prefix, which carries the sense of "to seek or ask for" something — so from the root "to forgive" it becomes "to seek forgiveness." It is a command to a single male ("you"), ending in the jussive (no final vowel).…
غ-ف-ر (gh-f-r) — to cover, conceal, pardon; hence forgiveness
and ask Him for forgiveness — seek that He cover over and wipe away shortcomings. Even at the moment of triumph, the servant turns to seek pardon, modeling humility rather than self-congratulation.

"inna" is a particle of emphasis and affirmation placed at the start of a statement to assert it firmly — best rendered "indeed" or "truly." It grammatically takes a following noun/pronoun in the accusative; here that is the attached pronoun "-hu" ("He"), referring to Allah. Together: "Indeed He..."

Truly He — "inna" is an emphasis word with no single English equivalent; it stresses the certainty of what follows, like saying "indeed, it is a fact that..." The attached "He" refers to Allah, setting up the reassurance in the rest of the verse.

"kāna" is the past-tense verb "to be," third-person singular masculine ("He was/has been"). The final "-a" is its normal past-tense ending. Applied to Allah it expresses permanence — an unchanging attribute — so it is best read as "He has ever been" / "He always is."
ك-و-ن (k-w-n) — to be, to exist, to come to be

has always been / ever is — "kāna" reports a settled, enduring reality. Used of Allah it does not mean a past that ended; it conveys that this is His constant, timeless nature: He has ever been and remains so.
ever-accepting of repentance / oft-relenting (accusative ending, completing "He has ever been ___")

This is an intensive noun-pattern (faʿʿāl), which signals something done abundantly and repeatedly — hence "ever / oft" accepting repentance, not merely "accepting" once. The final "-an" (tanwīn) is the accusative ending because it is the complement of "kāna" ("He has ever BEEN ever-relenting");…
ت-و-ب (t-w-b) — to turn back, return; repentance (from the servant) and acceptance of it / relenting (from Allah)

ever-relenting and ever-accepting of repentance — the One who repeatedly turns toward His servants in mercy, welcomes their return again and again, and accepts their repentance. The form is intensive: not a one-time act but a constant, abundant readiness to forgive.

surahs.name: المَسَد
surahs.why: Short narrative verses with past-tense verbs (تبّ، أغنى، كسب) — a story you already know, told in five lines.

verses.en: Ruin seize the hands of Abu Lahab—and may he himself perish—he who mocked the Message publicly and thought his tribe could protect him from consequences.
words: to perish, be ruined, come to nothing feminine-singular subject ending (agreeing with 'hands')

A past-tense verb (form I) used as a curse/invocation: 'may it perish!' The ending -at (ـَتْ) marks the subject as third-person feminine singular. It is feminine singular — not plural — because the grammatical subject is yadā ('the two hands');…
ت-ب-ب (t-b-b) — to perish, be ruined, suffer loss

May it perish / be ruined / come to nothing. A curse-prayer asking that the two hands of Abu Lahab — and through them the whole person and all his works — be destroyed and rendered utterly fruitless. It carries the sense of loss, failure, and ruin all at once. The verb is feminine singular because its subject is the pair of hands, which Arabic treats as a single feminine unit; natural English renders this 'may the hands perish.'

This is the dual form of yad ('hand'), so it means exactly 'two hands,' not one or many. It is the subject of the verb 'perish.' The dual normally ends in -āni, but because it is joined ('in construct') to the next word 'Abu (Lahab)' — 'the two hands OF Abu Lahab' — the final -ni drops, leaving just yadā.

The two hands. Literally a dual ('a pair of hands'), but here the hands stand for the whole person and all his deeds and efforts — what someone 'does' is done with the hands, so cursing the hands curses the man and everything he strives for.

This is one of a small set of 'five nouns' that show their case with a long vowel. Here the -ī ending is the genitive (possessive) form, signalling two links at once: 'the hands OF Abu…' (it is possessed by 'hands') and 'Abu OF Lahab' (it owns the next word). So abī = 'father of,' set up to attach to 'Lahab.'

Of Abu (literally 'father of'). 'Abu Lahab' is a nickname meaning 'Father of Flame,' given for his glowing, ruddy face; the Qur'an uses the nickname rather than his real name ('Abd al-'Uzza), and the 'flame' in it grimly foreshadows the Fire mentioned later.
flame, blaze the '-in' ending: genitive + indefinite (no 'the')

The final -in (written with the double-vowel mark called tanwīn) does two things: the -i part is genitive because this word is possessed by 'Abu' ('father OF Lahab'), and the -n part marks it as indefinite (no 'the'). Together they give the reading 'Father of Flame.'

Lahab — literally 'flame' or 'blaze.' As part of the name 'Abu Lahab' ('Father of Flame') it functions as a proper name, but the underlying meaning 'flame' is deliberately chosen and echoes the 'flaming Fire' he is warned of in the next verses.

'wa' (وَ) means 'and.' 'tabba' is the same form I past-tense verb as in word 1, but here it is third-person masculine singular, so the subject is Abu Lahab himself rather than 'the hands.' Masculine singular is shown by the absence of any subject suffix —…
ت-ب-ب (t-b-b) — to perish, be ruined, suffer loss

And he himself has perished / is ruined. Where the first verb was a prayer ('may it perish'), this closing statement declares it accomplished as a certain fact: he himself is utterly ruined. The shift from invocation to flat statement makes the doom sound sealed.

verses.ar: مَآ أَغۡنَىٰ عَنۡهُ مَالُهُۥ وَمَا كَسَبَ
verses.en: His wealth and whatever he accumulated will not rescue him when the account is due—no inheritance, no status, no emergency fund can pay that debt.
words: A standalone negative particle, here negating the past-tense verb that follows. It has no case ending of its own; it simply turns 'availed' into 'did NOT avail.' The lengthening on the alif is a recitation feature, not a grammatical ending.
A negating particle meaning "not" / "did not." Here it introduces a flat denial about the past: nothing whatsoever was of any use to him. In context it carries the force of "It availed him nothing."
availed / warded off (Form IV) he/it (3rd-person masc. past ending)

A past-tense verb, 3rd-person masculine singular ('it availed'). It is Form IV (formed with a prefixed hamza, اَ-, on the root), which makes the verb causative/transitive: 'to make someone free of need,' i.e.…
غ-ن-ي (gh-n-y) — to be free of need, richness, sufficiency
To avail, to be of use, to ward off need or harm. The sense is that his wealth could not enrich him, protect him, or save him from punishment — it could not make him independent of Allah's judgement.
from / on behalf of him (attached pronoun)

A preposition ('ʿan') with an attached pronoun ('-hu' = him). The pronoun is in the object form required after a preposition. Together they tell us the wealth did not avail 'for/from him' — pinning the benefit that never came to Abu Lahab specifically.
A two-part word meaning "from him" / "on his behalf," i.e. "for him." It marks the person for whose benefit (in fact, to no benefit) the availing was directed.

A noun ('māl' = wealth) plus the attached possessive pronoun '-hu' (his). The -u ending on 'mālu' is the nominative case, marking this as the doer/subject of the verb 'availed' — it is 'his wealth' that did the (non-)availing.
His wealth, his property and riches — the money and possessions he relied on and boasted of. This is the grammatical subject: the thing that failed to help him.

Two particles: 'wa' (and) joining this to 'his wealth,' and 'mā' here used not as a negative but as a relative pronoun meaning 'what / that which.' So the second subject of 'availed' is 'and that which he earned.' These particles take no case ending.
"And what..." — a connector joining a second subject to the first. It links 'his wealth' with the further thing that failed him, introducing 'whatever he earned/acquired.'

A past-tense verb, 3rd-person masculine singular: 'he earned.' The final -a is the standard past-tense ending for 'he (did).' It sits inside the relative clause begun by 'mā,' giving 'that which he earned.' Together with 'his wealth,' it forms the compound subject of 'availed him nothing.'

What he earned, gained, or acquired — the fruits of his striving. Classical commentators note this also covers his children, since in Arabic one's offspring are counted among one's 'earnings.' Neither his gains nor his sons could rescue him.

verses.en: He will enter a Fire of blazing flame—
words: shall / will soon (future marker) he (3rd-person masculine prefix) roast / be burned by fire

A present/future verb made future by the prefix sa- ('will, shall soon'). The ya- prefix marks a single male subject: 'he'. The verb ends in a long -ā (written with alif maqṣūra) because its final root letter is weak; it carries the indicative meaning of a real, certain event. Sense: 'he will surely be burned/roasted.'
ص-ل-ي (ṣ-l-y) — to roast, burn, be scorched by fire

He will soon roast in, be burned by, and scorched by the Fire. The verb describes being seared and consumed by fire, suffering its full heat. The attached future marker stresses that this is a certain, impending outcome awaiting him.

A noun in the accusative case, shown by the final -an (tanwīn fatḥa). The accusative marks it as the object/goal of the verb — what he will be burned in. The -an ending also signals that the noun is indefinite: 'a fire.'
ن-و-ر (n-w-r) — the sense of burning/blazing fire; the noun نَار ('fire') belongs to this root (distinct from نُور, 'light,' from the same root)
A fire — here the Fire of punishment in the Hereafter. It is left grammatically indefinite, which in this context magnifies it: 'a fire' of a terrible, indescribable kind.

The feminine form of dhū ('possessor of'). It is in the accusative case, shown by the final -a, because it describes the accusative noun 'fire' (nār is feminine, so the feminine dhāt agrees with it). It is grammatically bound to the word after it, meaning 'having ___ / possessor of ___.'
— (a possessive word; no verbal triliteral root)
Possessor of / having / full of. This word means 'one that possesses' a quality; here it links the Fire to its defining feature, describing it as a fire that owns and is characterized by flame.

A noun in the genitive case, shown by the final -in (tanwīn kasra). It is genitive because it follows dhāta ('possessor of'), forming a possessive link (iḍāfa): 'possessor OF flame' = 'full of flame.' The -in ending also marks it as indefinite.
ل-ه-ب (l-h-b) — to blaze, flame up; flame

Flame — the leaping, blazing tongues of fire. Describing the Fire as 'possessor of flame' paints it as fiercely blazing. The word also echoes the name Abu Lahab ('father of flame'), tying his fate to the very thing his nickname evoked.

verses.en: and his wife too, the carrier of thorns—she who spread harm alongside him, fueling hostility against the truth.
words: Starts with the connecting word "and" (wa-), joining this verse to what precedes.…
م-ر-ء (m-r-ʾ) — person / human being; feminine form: woman, wife

"And his wife" — i.e. the wife of Abu Lahab, Umm Jamil. The opening "and" (waw) connects this verse to the previous ones: she shares in the punishment alongside her husband. The word for "wife" here is the ordinary word for "woman," used in the sense of a man's spouse, with "his" pointing back to Abu Lahab.
This is an intensive doer-form (the faʿʿaal pattern, with the middle letter doubled) made feminine by the ـة ending — "one who carries a great deal." The doubling stresses repetition and intensity.…
ح-م-ل (ḥ-m-l) — to carry, bear, haul a load

"The carrier (of)" — a feminine intensive form describing someone who habitually and abundantly carries a load. The doubled, heavy pattern conveys that she does this constantly and zealously: she is the wood-hauler. It also carries a figurative sense in tafsir — one who spreads slander, "carrying tales" — and a literal sense of one who carried thorns to harm the Prophet (peace be upon him), and who will carry firewood to feed the Fire in the Hereafter.

Begins with the definite article al- ("the"). The stem "ḥaṭab" (firewood) carries the ending -i (genitive): the -i marks it as the thing possessed by "carrier," giving the meaning "carrier OF the firewood" — the genitive link is exactly what English shows with "of."
ح-ط-ب (ḥ-ṭ-b) — firewood, to gather wood for fuel

"Of the firewood" — the dry wood/kindling that she carries. Literally the fuel gathered for burning. Commentators explain it both literally (firewood, or the thorns she strewed in the Prophet's path) and figuratively (the "fuel" of slander she carried between people), and as the wood she will bear in Hell.

verses.en: Around her neck will be a rope of palm fibre—a symbol of the burden she chose and the humiliation that follows arrogance against Allah.
words: An indeclinable preposition (a fixed little word that never changes its form). Its job is to point to a location: "in/on her neck." Whatever noun comes after it must take the genitive ending — which is exactly why the next word reads jīdi-hā (with an -i) rather than jīdu-hā.

A locational particle meaning "in," "inside," or "upon." Here it sets the scene of the punishment by placing what follows (a rope) physically on a specific spot — her neck. It governs the word after it, putting it into the genitive case.

A noun followed by an attached possessive pronoun. The -i ending on jīdi is genitive, caused by the preposition fī before it ("in her neck"). The suffix -hā means "her," referring to a single female; because this pronoun is possessing the noun, the two together form an "of" relationship — literally "the neck of her."

Her neck — specifically the neck or throat region where a necklace or collar would sit. The reference is to the wife of Abu Lahab, and the image is grimly ironic: the place she once adorned with jewellery now bears a rope of torment.

A singular noun carrying the ending -un, a form of tanwīn (nunation) written above the final letter as two small ḍamma strokes (tiny curly hooks), not as two dots. That doubled-vowel ending marks the word as indefinite —…

A rope or cord — a length of twisted strands used for binding or hanging. Left deliberately indefinite ("a rope"), which in Arabic can suggest something dreadful and beyond description. It is the subject of the sentence: a rope is/will be on her neck.

An indeclinable preposition (a fixed word that does not change form). It governs the following noun and forces it into the genitive case — which is why the next word ends in -in. In this context it conveys "made of / consisting of," describing the rope's material.

A particle meaning "of," "from," or "made out of." Here it expresses material or origin — the rope is made from / consists of the substance named next. It links the rope to what it is fashioned out of.

A singular noun with the ending -in, a form of tanwīn (nunation) written beneath the final letter as two small kasra strokes. The -i part is the genitive case, required because the preposition min comes before it ("of palm-fibre"). The tanwīn (the doubled vowel) marks it as indefinite —…
م-س-د (m-s-d) — to twist / tightly wind (a fibre rope)

Masad — palm-fibre: a coarse rope twisted from the fibres of the date palm. Classical commentators also explain it can mean a tightly twisted cord, or, in the Fire, a chain of iron. The word is left indefinite to heighten its harshness. There is deliberate sound-play with "lahab" (flame) earlier in the surah.

surahs.name: قُرَيْش
surahs.why: Paired with Al-Fil in recitation — worship the Lord of this House, who feeds against hunger and secures against fear. You will stand at 'this House'.

verses.en: For the accustomed security of Quraysh—
words: for / because of (a preposition giving the reason or purpose) making accustomed, habituation, granting settled familiarity (the final '-i' here is the genitive case vowel required by the preposition 'li', not a separate word-part)

The 'li' prefix is a preposition meaning 'for' or 'because of'; it both gives a reason (in amazement at, or in gratitude for, Allah's favor) and, on another reading, points forward to the command in ayah 3 ('let them worship...').…
ء-ل-ف (ʾ-l-f) — to be familiar, intimate, accustomed; to join/unite in harmony

For the familiarity and habituation of — that is, for the settled custom and accustomedness that Allah granted. The verbal noun ilāf means making something familiar and habitual so that it becomes a settled practice. The full word means 'for the (granting of) familiarity/habituation.' (The safety and prosperity that resulted from this — for example the secure trade caravans of ayah 2 — were a consequence of the familiarity, not part of the word's own meaning.)
Quraysh, the tribe of Makkah (the final '-in' is the genitive case ending with nunation, marking it as the possessor of the preceding 'īlāf', not a separate word-part)

This is a proper name (the tribe Quraysh) and the second term of a possessive construction (iḍāfah): 'the familiarity/habituation OF Quraysh', so it stands in the genitive case. In the Quranic text it is read 'qurayshin', with the genitive marked by nunation (tanwīn, the final 'n').…
ق-ر-ش (q-r-sh) — to gather, collect, earn; 'Quraysh' is commonly explained as a diminutive form from this root (and is also linked to qirsh, a sea creature/shark)

Of the Quraysh — the leading Arab tribe of Makkah, the custodians of the Sacred House (the Kaʿbah) and the tribe of the Prophet. The word names this specific people; the genitive ending shows it is the possessor: the familiarity/habituation 'of Quraysh.'

verses.en: Their accustomed trading journeys in winter to Yemen and summer to Syria—safe passage others did not enjoy—
words: making accustomed / habitual familiarity (the -i ending: 'of', genitive linking vowel) their (them)
This is a verbal noun (maṣdar) from form IV (the 'a-/ī- causative' pattern), so it carries the sense of 'causing to become familiar / making habituated', not merely 'being familiar'.…
أ-ل-ف (ʾ-l-f) — to be familiar with, to grow accustomed, to join together in intimacy

Their being made accustomed and habituated — the deep-seated familiarity, attachment, and settled habit of the Quraysh with regard to their seasonal trade journeys. The word is a verbal noun (the act/state itself) bound to the pronoun 'their', and it restates and explains the 'familiarity (īlāf)' mentioned in the previous verse.
a journey / caravan trip (feminine noun stem; the ة is pronounced -t- here) (the -a ending on the ة: object marker)

The noun stem رِحۡلَة ends in a tāʾ marbūṭa (ة), the feminine marker that is part of the word itself; it marks a single instance of the act ('one journey'). In connected speech and in this possessive construction the ة is sounded as -t-, and the case vowel sits on it, so the word reads riḥla-t-a.…
ر-ح-ل (r-ḥ-l) — to depart, set out, journey; a mount/saddle for travel
A journey or expedition — specifically the organized trade caravan/trek that the Quraysh would set out on each season. It denotes a single act of travelling/setting off, not travel in general.

The prefix al- is 'the' (the 'l' is silent here and doubles the following 'sh' — pronounced ash-shitāʾ). The final vowel is -i (l-shitāi), the genitive case, because it is possessed by the previous word: 'the journey OF the winter'. So this word links back to riḥla to mean 'the winter journey'.
The winter — the cold season. According to the tafsir, this was the caravan the Quraysh sent to the warm south (Yemen) during the cold months.

The prefix wa- means 'and', joining this to 'the winter'. The prefix al- is 'the' (the 'l' is silent and doubles the 'ṣ' — pronounced aṣ-ṣayf). The final vowel is -i (wal-ṣayfi), the genitive case, matching 'the winter': it too is possessed by 'the journey', giving 'the journey of winter AND summer'.
And the summer — the hot season. This was the second caravan, sent north to Syria (Ash-Shām) during the warm months. The verse pairs the two seasons to capture the Quraysh's year-round trade.

verses.en: Let them worship the Rabb of this House—the Ka'bah through which their safety and livelihood flowed—
words: so / then (a connector drawing a conclusion) let / must (command particle) they worship (silent spelling alif after the plural -ū)

The fa- links this back to the earlier favours ('so, in response...'). The la- is the 'command' particle (lam al-amr) that turns a statement into an order aimed at a third party ('let them...'). Following the connector fa-, this lam takes no vowel of its own (sukun) and is read straight on as fal-yaʿbudū —…
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, serve, and submit in devotion

A command framed as a consequence: "so let them worship / they must worship." It draws a conclusion from the favours just mentioned (security and provision) — since Allah did all that for them, the proper response is that they devote their worship to Him alone. The verb means to serve and worship with humble, exclusive devotion.

This is the object of 'worship' — the One they are commanded to worship. The final -a ending is the accusative case, which marks it as the direct object ('worship the Lord'). It is also the first term of a possessive construction (idafa) with the following words, so it reads 'the Lord OF this House.'
ر-ب-ب (r-b-b) — to nurture, sustain, master, and bring to completion
The Lord and Master — the One who creates, owns, sustains, and lovingly nurtures all that exists. Here it specifically presents Allah as the Owner and Sustainer of the Sacred House (the Ka'bah).

A demonstrative ('this') referring to a single masculine thing that is near. It is indeclinable, so it keeps the same form regardless of case; it simply points to 'the House' that follows and ties the phrase together as 'this House.'
A pointing word, 'this' — singling out one specific, near thing: 'this House' right here, the Ka'bah they live beside.
The al- prefix is 'the,' marking it as a specific, well-known house (the Ka'bah). The -i ending is the genitive case.…
ب-ي-ت (b-y-t) — to spend the night, dwell; hence bayt, a house or dwelling
The House — meaning the Sacred House, the Ka'bah in Makkah, the first house of worship built for the One God and the focal point of the Quraysh's honour, safety, and livelihood.

verses.ar: ٱلَّذِيٓ أَطۡعَمَهُم مِّن جُوعٖ وَءَامَنَهُم مِّنۡ خَوۡفِۭ
verses.en: Who fed them against hunger and secured them against fear.
words: A masculine singular relative pronoun ('the one who'). It is built (indeclinable) so its ending never changes for case. Here it functions as a description of Allah, linking back to 'the Lord of this House' and setting up the verbs that come next.

A relative pronoun meaning 'the One Who' / 'He Who'. It refers back to 'the Lord of this House' (Allah) in the previous ayah and introduces the description that follows, presenting Him as the One who does the actions of feeding and securing.

A past-tense (form IV, causative) verb: the a- prefix with this pattern turns 'to eat' into 'to make eat / to feed'. The verb is third-person masculine singular ('He'), referring to Allah; the final -a is the normal past-tense ending. The attached -hum ('them') is the object — the people being fed.
He fed them / He provided them with food, satisfying their hunger. The verb conveys actively giving sustenance, and the attached 'them' marks the Quraysh as the recipients of this provision.

A preposition ('from'). It is built (indeclinable) and does not change. It governs the noun that follows ('hunger'), putting it in the genitive case; in context it expresses removal from or protection against that condition.
A preposition meaning 'from'. Here it carries the sense of 'against' or 'on account of' — feeding them so as to relieve them from a state of hunger.
An indefinite noun ('hunger'). The final -in ending (tanwīn, the 'n' sound) marks it as indefinite ('hunger / a state of hunger') and as genitive because it follows the preposition 'from'.
Hunger — the painful want of food and the famine-like need the Quraysh would have faced without Allah's provision.
and he made safe / gave security them

The wa- prefix means 'and', joining this to the previous verb. The verb itself is past tense, form IV (causative): 'to make safe / to give security' (built from the root meaning 'to be safe'). Form IV normally takes a prefixed hamza on the pattern afʿala, which here would give aʾmana (a- prefix + root-initial hamza).…
And He made them safe / gave them security, removing their fear. The verb conveys granting safety and peace of mind, with the attached 'them' again marking the Quraysh as those made secure.

A preposition ('from'). It is built (indeclinable) and does not change. It governs the following noun ('fear'), putting it in the genitive case; here it conveys protection or safety from that condition.
A preposition meaning 'from'. As in the parallel phrase above, it carries the sense of 'against' — securing them from a state of fear.
An indefinite noun ('fear'). The final -in ending (tanwīn, the 'n' sound) marks it as indefinite ('fear / a state of fear') and as genitive because it follows the preposition 'from'.
Fear — dread of danger, attack, or harm. Set opposite 'hunger', it completes the pair of essential blessings: food for the body and safety for life.

surahs.name: الفِيل
surahs.why: The army that came for the Kaaba — five verses of vivid past-tense narrative (أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ).

verses.ar: أَلَمۡ تَرَ كَيۡفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِأَصۡحَٰبِ ٱلۡفِيلِ
verses.en: Have you not seen how your Lord dealt with the army of the elephant—when worldly power marched against His House, confident that sheer force decides history?
words: Two particles joined together. The a- at the front turns the statement into a question. lam is a negative particle that puts the verb after it into the jussive mood and pushes its meaning into the past, so 'lam tara' = 'you did not see / have not seen'.…

A compound interrogative meaning 'Have you not...?' It fuses the question-marker a- ('do/did...?') with lam ('not'). Rather than seeking information, it is a rhetorical question used to draw attention and affirm a known truth: 'Surely you are aware how...' It invites the listener to recall and reflect on something certain.
'you' (the doer; masculine singular) the verb stem 'see'; its middle root-letter hamza (ء) and its final weak root-letter ( y) have both dropped out on the surface, leaving just 'ra'

A present-tense verb governed by the preceding 'lam', which puts it in the jussive mood and shifts its time-frame to the past: 'you have (not) seen'. The ta- prefix marks the subject as 'you' (one male person).…

'You see/saw' — here in the sense of perceiving, witnessing, or coming to know with certainty. The seeing is not only with the eyes but with the understanding: 'have you not come to realise / take note of'. The one addressed is the Prophet (peace be upon him), and through him every listener.

An indeclinable question-word (it never changes its form for case). It fixes permanently on a final '-a' (fatḥah) ending. It introduces and is the object of the verb that follows ('how He dealt'), framing the entire clause as a question about manner.

'How' — an interrogative asking about the manner or way in which something was done. Here it carries amazement: it points to the striking, decisive manner in which the Lord dealt with the aggressors, inviting wonder rather than a literal answer.

A plain past-tense verb in its base form (form I). With no attached subject pronoun, the bare stem with its '-a' ending built in means 'he did' — a single male doer, here 'your Lord'. The final '-a' is simply the standard vowel of the past-tense verb. The subject ('your Lord') comes right after as a separate word.

'He did / acted / dealt with' — a general verb of doing. In this context it means how the Lord 'handled' or 'dealt with' the people of the elephant, i.e. the action He took against them. It deliberately understates a momentous event, letting the following verses spell out the 'how'.

A noun followed by an attached possessive pronoun. The '-u' on 'rabbu' is the nominative ending, marking it as the subject (the doer) of the verb 'faʿala' — it is your Lord who did this. The attached '-ka' means 'your', addressing one male person. Together: 'your Lord' as the actor of the sentence.

'Your Lord' — 'Rabb' is far richer than 'master': the One who creates, owns, sustains, nurtures, and brings each thing to its completion, caring for it at every stage. Attached to 'you' (the Prophet), it is intimate and reassuring: the very Lord who looks after you is the One who acted thus.
A preposition 'bi-' ('with') joined to a plural noun. 'aṣḥāb' is the broken (irregular) plural of 'ṣāḥib' ('companion').…

'With the companions/owners of...' — 'aṣḥāb' are the people closely associated with a thing, its companions, masters, or owners; here 'the people of the elephant', i.e. Abraha's army that marched on the Sacred House with an elephant. The opening 'bi-' ('with') links the verb to them: how He dealt 'with' them.
The definite article 'al-' ('the') attached to the noun 'fīl' ('elephant'). The 'a' of 'al-' is dropped in pronunciation after the preceding word, so it sounds like 'l-fīli'.…

'The elephant' — referring to the great war-elephant (and the host that brought it) used by Abraha in the failed attempt to destroy the Kaʿbah. The whole army took its name from this beast, which famously refused to advance on the Sacred House.

verses.en: Did He not turn their plot into failure—exposing that no coalition, no technology of its age, no arrogance, outruns His decree?
words: a question marker: 'did...?' / 'is it not that...?' 'not' — a particle that negates a verb and shifts it to past meaning

This is a question particle (a-) fused with the negating particle lam. Lam is special: it takes the jussive form of the present-tense verb that follows, yet gives it a PAST meaning. So 'a-lam yajʿal' literally is 'did-not-he-make' = 'Did He not make...?' The expected answer is an emphatic 'yes, He certainly did.'

A two-part opening that asks a pointed question expecting the answer 'yes, of course.' It combines the question-word 'a- ('did...?') with lam ('not'), so together it means 'Did He not...?' — a rhetorical question inviting the listener to affirm what Allah did. Because lam pushes a present-tense verb back into the past, the whole clause refers to a completed past event.
'he' — the prefix marking a 3rd-person masculine singular subject 'make / place / render / cause to be'

A present-tense verb form (Form I) carrying the 'he' prefix ya-. Although the shape is present-tense, it sits under lam from the previous word, so it is jussive in form and PAST in meaning — note the bare, vowelless ending -l (sukūn), the tell-tale sign of the jussive after lam. Hence 'Did He not make...'.…
ج-ع-ل (j-ʿ-l) — to make, place, render, cause to become

'He placed / He made / He rendered' — here in the sense of causing their plot to END UP in a particular state. With the preposition that follows, it means 'He turned/put their scheme into (a state of) ruin.' The verb refers to Allah's single decisive act in the past.
'plot, scheme, cunning stratagem' 'their' — attached pronoun for 'them' (masculine plural)

A noun (kayd) with the possessive pronoun -hum ('their') joined to its end. The noun carries the -a ending (kayda), which is the accusative case: it marks kayd as the direct object of the verb 'make' — i.e. the thing that He placed into ruin. The suffix -hum points back to the people of the Elephant.
ك-ي-د (k-y-d) — to plot, scheme, devise a cunning plan

'Their plot / their scheme / their cunning plan' — the malicious stratagem of the army of the Elephant to destroy the Sacred House. The word kayd denotes crafty, hostile plotting; the attached 'their' identifies the plotters (Abraha and his men).

A preposition meaning 'in/into.' It governs the noun that follows, forcing it into the genitive case (hence the -in ending on the next word). Together 'make ... fī ...' expresses turning the plot into a state of ruin.
'In / into' — a small linking word. Paired with the verb 'make,' it carries the sense of causing something to end up 'in(to)' a state: 'He put their plot INTO...' a condition of loss.
'causing to go astray / leading into ruin and loss' the '-in' ending: genitive case + nunation (an unspecified 'a state of...')
A verbal noun (masdar) of Form II on the tafʿīl pattern (the ta- prefix with a doubled middle radical, taḍlīl).…
ض-ل-ل (ḍ-l-l) — to go astray, lose the way, be lost; (Form II) to lead astray, render void

'Leading astray / causing to go astray / rendering futile' — a causative action: their scheme was MADE to go nowhere, derailed and brought to nothing. This is the verbal noun of the transitive/causative Form II verb (ḍallala, 'to lead astray, to render void'), not the intransitive 'straying' of Form I. So 'He placed their plot fī taḍlīl' means Allah caused their plot to go astray, i.e. He thwarted it and made it utterly fail. The Form II pattern adds an intensive force, stressing how thoroughly their plan was made to come to nothing.

verses.en: He sent against them flocks of birds—
words: A Form IV (af'ala, the 'a-' prefix on the stem) past-tense verb, giving the sense 'to send out / dispatch.' It has no visible subject pronoun; the bare 3rd-person masculine singular form means 'He (did)' — here referring to Allah. The final '-a' is the normal past-tense ending.…
ر-س-ل (r-s-l) — to send, dispatch (the same root as rasūl, 'messenger')

And He sent (forth / against). A past-tense verb meaning that Allah dispatched or released something and sent it out toward a target. The opening 'and' connects this act to the previous verse, continuing the account of what Allah did to the Army of the Elephant.
The preposition 'ʿalā' ('on, upon, against') takes the special form 'ʿalay-' before an attached pronoun.…
— (preposition with attached pronoun; no triliteral root)

Upon them / against them. A preposition meaning 'on' or 'over' joined to a pronoun referring to the people of the Elephant — so 'against them,' conveying that the birds were sent down upon them as a punishment from above.

A collective noun meaning 'birds.' The final '-an' (written with tanwīn, the doubled mark) is the indefinite accusative ending: it marks the word as the direct object of 'He sent' ('He sent birds') and as indefinite ('some birds,' not 'the birds'). The accusative '-a-' vowel is what shows it is the thing being sent.
Birds. A collective noun for flying creatures (birds in general), here the creatures Allah sent against the invading army.

A plural noun (with no commonly used singular) meaning 'flocks / successive swarms.' Grammarians most often parse it as a ḥāl — a circumstantial accusative describing the state in which the birds came ('He sent birds, [coming] in flocks') —…
— (no agreed triliteral root; an Arabic plural-form word for teeming, successive groups)

In flocks — in successive, swarming groups coming wave after wave from every direction. The word describes the birds as arriving not as a single flight but as many companies following one upon another.

verses.en: striking them with stones of baked clay—
words: she / it (feminine subject prefix — referring back to the flock of birds, ṭayr, treated as feminine) throws / pelts / hurls them (object pronoun, masculine plural — the attackers)
A present/imperfect verb (Form I), 3rd person feminine singular ('she/it'). The ta- prefix marks the feminine subject, pointing back to the flock of birds.…

"It (the flock of birds) keeps pelting / striking them." A present-tense verb picturing the birds continually hurling down stones upon the army of the elephant. The vivid ongoing tense makes the scene live before the listener, as though the assault is unfolding now.

A prefix bi- ('with, by means of') showing the instrument, attached to the noun ḥijārah ('stones'). Because bi- always puts the following noun in the genitive case, the ending appears as -in (ḥijāratin). The -in is also nunation (tanwīn), marking the noun as indefinite — 'some stones,' not 'the stones.'
"With stones." The instrument of the punishment: hard stones (each bird carried small baked-clay pebbles) flung down with deadly force.

An indeclinable preposition ('of, from'). It does not change its form, but it governs the word after it, putting that following noun into the genitive case. Here it links the stones to their material: stones 'of' baked clay.…
"Of / made from." A small linking particle here meaning 'consisting of' or 'made out of' — it tells you what the stones were composed of.

A single noun with no prefixes or suffixes. Its ending is -in (sijjīlin): the genitive case, required because it follows the preposition min ('of'). The -in is also nunation (tanwīn), marking it as indefinite — 'baked clay' in general. So the phrase reads 'stones OF baked clay.'
— (commonly held to be of Persian origin — per Ibn Hisham, from Sanj 'stone' + Jil 'clay' — Arabized; not a native triliteral root)

"Baked clay / hardened stone of clay." Sijjīl denotes hard lumps of clay baked into stone — fired, rock-hard pellets. Commentators note it conveys something hard and solid, sent as a decree of punishment.

verses.en: and left them like eaten straw—power reduced to chaff, a lesson for every empire that thinks it is permanent.
words: and so / then (marks the result or sequence of events) He made / turned (into) them (object)

Built on the prefix fa- ("and so/then"), which signals that this is the consequence of what came before. The stem jaʿala is a past-tense verb with no visible subject prefix or suffix, which is the default form for "he" — so the doer is "He" (Allah).…
ج-ع-ل (j-ʿ-l) — to make, to render, to turn something into something

"Then He made them / rendered them (into something)," referring to Allah reducing the army of the Elephant to a ruined state. The verb here is transformative: it does not mean merely "made" in the sense of creating, but "turned them into" or "reduced them to" the condition described next.
like / as chaff, dry straw, crop husks

The prefix ka- means "like/as" and is a particle that forces the following noun into the genitive case — which is exactly why the noun ends in -in. That -in ending is genitive plus tanwin (the "-n" of indefiniteness), so it reads "like (some) chaff," indefinite. So the two cues together —…
ع-ص-ف (ʿ-ṣ-f) — dry plant matter; chaff/straw (also linked to the sense of a violent, scattering wind)

"Like chaff / like dry stalks and leaves of a crop." ʿaṣf refers to the withered husks, straw, and stripped foliage left over from grain plants — worthless, crushed plant debris. The prefixed "like" makes it a vivid comparison for the shattered, trampled remains of the army.
eaten, devoured (base of the passive participle) final root letter + indefinite genitive ending (-in)

This is a passive participle of form I — the pattern mafʿūl, here maʾkūl, meaning "that which is eaten / having been eaten" (passive, describing the receiver of the action). In the source script the hamza of the root is written as an alif (مَّاۡكُو…) and the initial mim carries a shadda (مَّ);…
أ-ك-ل (ʾ-k-l) — to eat, to consume, to devour

"Eaten / devoured," describing the chaff as already consumed — chewed and spat out by grazing animals, or eaten through. The image is of plant refuse that has been bitten into and reduced to fragments, underscoring how utterly the army was destroyed.

surahs.name: آيَة الكُرْسِيّ
surahs.why: The greatest verse in the Quran — recited after every salah and before sleep. One long verse, and you already know half its words: الله، لا، إلا، هو، الحي، ما، في، السماوات، الأرض، من، عند، يعلم.

verses.ar: ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلۡحَيُّ ٱلۡقَيُّومُۚ لَا تَأۡخُذُهُۥ سِنَةٞ وَلَا نَوۡمٞۚ لَّهُۥ مَا فِي ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِي ٱلۡأَرۡضِۗ مَن ذَا ٱلَّذِي يَشۡفَعُ عِندَهُۥٓ إِلَّا بِإِذۡنِهِۦۚ يَعۡلَمُ مَا بَيۡنَ أَيۡدِيهِمۡ وَمَا خَلۡفَهُمۡۖ وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَيۡءٖ مِّنۡ عِلۡمِهِۦٓ إِلَّا بِمَا شَآءَۚ وَسِعَ كُرۡسِيُّهُ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلۡأَرۡضَۖ وَلَا يَـُٔودُهُۥ حِفۡظُهُمَاۚ وَهُوَ ٱلۡعَلِيُّ ٱلۡعَظِيمُ
verses.en: Allah — there is no god worthy of worship except Him, the Ever-Living, All-Sustaining. Neither drowsiness nor sleep overtakes Him. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on earth. Who can intercede with Him except by His permission? He knows what is before them and what is behind them, and they grasp none of His knowledge except what He wills. His Kursi — the Footstool symbolizing His dominion over creation — encompasses the heavens and earth, and their preservation does not weary Him. He is the Most High, the Greatest.
words: Allah (the divine name) nominative case ending (damma)
A proper noun (the divine name). Every letter of اللّٰه, including the final ه, is an integral consonant of the name; it is not built from separable affixes.…

The proper name Allah — the One True God, the unique and supreme Deity worshipped in Islam, without partner or equal. The name itself denotes the sole object of worship. Here it stands in the nominative case (as a subject or topic).

An indeclinable negating particle: it has no case, tense, or number and never changes shape. It works by governing the word after it — for instance driving a following indefinite noun to a bare form to express absolute negation ('there is no …').

A particle of negation, 'no' or 'not'. In one common usage it denies the existence of an entire category ('there is no … at all'); it also serves as a simple 'no' and can negate verbs in certain constructions. Its core function is to cancel or rule out what follows.
god / object of worship (accusative / 'absolute negation' ending)

A singular noun carrying an -a ending. This short -a typically marks the accusative (object) case; it is also the form a noun takes immediately after the absolute-negation particle 'lā' to mean 'no … (at all).'
أ-ل-ه (ʾ-l-h) — godhood, worship, being a deity
God / object of worship — a deity that is worshipped. The bare noun names the concept of a worshipped god.

An exceptive particle (originally a fusion of 'in' + 'lā'). It is invariable — it has no case ending and does not change form. It introduces the item being excepted from, or restricted within, the preceding statement.

A particle of exception meaning 'except,' 'save,' or 'only.' It carves out something from a preceding general statement — most often a preceding negation — so that the excepted item alone is affirmed, giving the strong sense 'none … but' or 'nothing other than.'

An independent (detached) personal pronoun, third-person masculine singular. It does not take case endings; its fixed final -a is part of the word. It serves to name or emphasize the subject ('He is the one who…').
'He' — the standalone (independent) pronoun for a single masculine person or thing, used for emphasis or to stand as the subject on its own.
the living / ever-living definite nominative ending (-u)
A definite adjective/noun ('the Living'). The -u ending is nominative, the case typically used for the subject of a sentence or for a noun in apposition (a descriptive title) to such a subject.

'the Ever-Living / the Living One' — possessing life in the fullest, absolute, and eternal sense: life that never began and never ends, the source of all life. As a divine name it affirms perfect, undying life.
the self-subsisting sustainer definite nominative ending (-u)

A definite noun in an intensive pattern (denoting permanence and totality). The -u ending is nominative, the case typically used for the subject of a sentence or for an appositive descriptive title attached to such a subject.
ق-و-م (q-w-m) — to stand, rise, maintain, uphold

'the Self-Subsisting Sustainer' — the One who exists wholly by Himself, needing nothing, and by whom all else is maintained and kept in being. It combines absolute self-existence with the constant upholding of everything that exists.

An indeclinable negating particle: it has no case, tense, or number and never changes shape. It works by governing the word after it — for instance driving a following indefinite noun to a bare form to express absolute negation ('there is no …').

A particle of negation, 'no' or 'not'. In one common usage it denies the existence of an entire category ('there is no … at all'); it also serves as a simple 'no' and can negate verbs in certain constructions. Its core function is to cancel or rule out what follows.
she / it (does) take / seize indicative ending (-u) him

A present-tense verb, third-person feminine singular ('she/it takes'), with attached object pronoun -hu ('him'). The -u before the suffix is the indicative ending, marking a plain, ongoing statement of fact ('it takes / does take').
'it takes/seizes Him' or 'overtakes Him' — to take, grasp, or lay hold of, with the object being 'Him.' The verb conveys something coming over or gripping a person.

A feminine singular noun (the -a / tā' marbūṭa marks the feminine). The -un ending is nominative and indefinite ('drowsiness'): the tanwīn marks indefiniteness, and the -u vowel typically marks it as the subject of a sentence.
'drowsiness / light slumber' — the heaviness or doze that precedes sleep, that first creeping of sleepiness over a person, as distinct from full sleep.
Two particles: the joining 'and' (wa) plus the negator lā ('not'). Lā commonly negates a present-tense verb or a noun. Neither particle takes case endings.
'And not' — a connector 'and' joined to the negative particle 'not,' negating a following statement.
A masculine singular noun. The -un ending is nominative and indefinite ('sleep'): the tanwīn (doubled-n) marks indefiniteness, and the -u vowel typically marks it as the subject of a sentence.
'sleep' — full, deep sleep; the state of being asleep, in contrast to mere drowsiness.

A preposition 'li-' ('to/for') fused with the attached pronoun 'him'. The pronoun 'him' stays the same; it is the preposition that changes, its vowel shifting from -i (li-) to -a (la-) when an attached pronoun follows it directly. The whole unit commonly conveys possession or that something is for someone's benefit.
For him / to him / belonging to him. A small preposition meaning 'to/for' joined to the pronoun 'him', expressing possession, benefit, or direction toward him.
A fixed (indeclinable) particle with no case ending; its sense is set by context. As a relative it means 'that which' and needs a following clause; as an interrogative it means 'what?'.

A versatile word most often meaning 'what' or 'that which' — it can introduce a relative idea ('the thing that…'), ask a question ('what?'), or in other uses serve as a negating particle. Its core job is to stand in for an unspecified thing.
A preposition. It is invariable (it does not change form) and places the following noun into the genitive case, indicating containment or location 'in / within' something.
A preposition meaning 'in,' 'within,' or 'inside.' It locates something inside a place, a time, a state, or a matter — physically or figuratively (e.g. 'in' a condition or 'concerning' a matter).

A definite feminine plural noun with the prefix 'al-' ('the'). The -i ending marks the genitive case, which typically links the word to a preceding noun ('… OF the heavens') or follows a preposition. In the sound feminine plural, this -i form also covers what would be an object role.
The heavens / the skies — the plural of 'heaven', referring to the skies above and the celestial realms; the elevated expanse over the earth.

The connector wa ('and') joined to mā ('what, that which'). Here mā functions as a relative/indefinite pronoun ('that which'); the same word can elsewhere serve as a negator. It is invariable and takes no case ending.
A connective phrase meaning 'and what' or 'and that which'. It adds a further item and points to an unspecified thing or category.
A preposition. It is invariable (it does not change form) and places the following noun into the genitive case, indicating containment or location 'in / within' something.
A preposition meaning 'in,' 'within,' or 'inside.' It locates something inside a place, a time, a state, or a matter — physically or figuratively (e.g. 'in' a condition or 'concerning' a matter).

A feminine singular noun with the definite article al- ('the'). The -i ending marks the genitive case, which typically links the word to a preceding noun ('... of the earth') or follows a preposition (as after 'in / on the earth').
The earth; the land, ground, or soil. The world we walk upon, as opposed to the sky.

An indeclinable pronoun (it never changes its ending) referring to a person or persons — 'who' or 'whoever'. It can ask a question ('who?') or, as a relative pronoun, link to a following clause that describes the person(s) it stands for.
A pronoun meaning 'who / whoever / the one who', used for people; it points to a person or persons without naming them and usually introduces a description of them.
A demonstrative pronoun ('this/that one'). It does not take case endings; the final -ā is part of the fixed word. It functions to point out or identify someone or something.
A demonstrative meaning 'this one' or 'the one' — a pointing word ('that/this') used to single out a person or thing. It often combines with the question particle to form 'who is it that…?'

A fixed (indeclinable) masculine singular relative pronoun: its form does not change for case, so the ending stays -ī whatever grammatical role it fills. It always needs a following clause to complete its meaning.

A relative pronoun meaning 'the one who' or 'which' — it points back to a masculine singular person or thing and introduces a clause that describes or identifies it. It works like the English 'who/which/that' that opens a defining clause.
A present-tense verb, third-person masculine singular ('he intercedes'). The final -u is the indicative ending, marking a plain statement of fact about an ongoing or general action.
'(he/it) intercedes' — to plead or mediate on another's behalf, interposing oneself to seek pardon or benefit for someone before a higher authority.

'ʿinda' is an adverb of place/possession that always governs a following word in the genitive; here it takes the attached pronoun -hu ('him/his'). The -a on 'ʿinda' is its fixed accusative-style ending as an adverbial. The pronoun -hu means the thing is located with, or owned by, a single male.

'With him / in his keeping / that he has' — combining the locational word 'ind ('at, with, in the presence/possession of') with the pronoun 'him'. It conveys that something is held by, present with, or belongs to him.

An exceptive particle (originally a fusion of 'in' + 'lā'). It is invariable — it has no case ending and does not change form. It introduces the item being excepted from, or restricted within, the preceding statement.

A particle of exception meaning 'except,' 'save,' or 'only.' It carves out something from a preceding general statement — most often a preceding negation — so that the excepted item alone is affirmed, giving the strong sense 'none … but' or 'nothing other than.'
with / by permission / leave genitive ending His

The prefix 'bi-' is a preposition meaning 'with' or 'by (means of)'. It places the following noun in the genitive case, shown by the '-i' ending. The attached '-hi' is a possessive pronoun, 'His' (or 'its'), so the whole reads 'by His permission'.
أ-ذ-ن (ʾ-dh-n) — to permit, give leave; to hear/heed

'By His permission / with His leave'. It joins 'with/by' to the noun 'permission, leave, sanction' and an attached pronoun 'His', indicating something done by the authorization of the one referred to.
Present-tense verb, third-person masculine singular ('he/it'). The ya- prefix marks 'he,' and the final -u marks the ordinary (indicative) present, 'he knows / does know.'
ع-ل-م (ʿ-l-m) — knowledge; knowing, being aware, recognizing
'He/it knows' — a present-tense verb of knowing: to have knowledge of, be aware of, recognize, or understand something.
A fixed (indeclinable) particle with no case ending; its sense is set by context. As a relative it means 'that which' and needs a following clause; as an interrogative it means 'what?'.

A versatile word most often meaning 'what' or 'that which' — it can introduce a relative idea ('the thing that…'), ask a question ('what?'), or in other uses serve as a negating particle. Its core job is to stand in for an unspecified thing.

An adverb of place ('between'). It always ends in -a and grammatically governs what follows it, putting the following noun(s) into the genitive 'of' relationship. It does not change to show 'the' or a case of its own; the fixed -a is simply how this word is formed.
ب-ي-ن (b-y-n) — separation, distinction, what lies between
Between; in the midst of two things. An adverb of place marking the space separating or lying among parties or points.

A plural noun with an attached possessive pronoun '-him' meaning 'their' (a group of males, or mixed). 'aydī' is the irregular ('broken') plural of 'yad' (hand). The pronoun ending makes the whole construction definite and possessed;…
Their hands. The plural noun 'hands' carrying the masculine plural possessive 'their'; often used idiomatically for what people do, produce, or are responsible for through their own deeds.

The connector wa ('and') joined to mā ('what, that which'). Here mā functions as a relative/indefinite pronoun ('that which'); the same word can elsewhere serve as a negator. It is invariable and takes no case ending.
A connective phrase meaning 'and what' or 'and that which'. It adds a further item and points to an unspecified thing or category.
An adverb of place ('behind') with the attached pronoun -hum ('them'). The -a ending is accusative, the case regularly used for adverbs of place and time, indicating the location 'behind.'
خ-ل-ف (kh-l-f) — to be behind, come after, succeed
'behind them' — the space or position to the rear of a group; also figuratively what lies after them, what awaits them, or what is hidden from them.

'wa' is 'and'. 'lā' is a negating particle; placed before a verb of command it expresses prohibition ('do not ...'), and before other verbs/nouns it expresses negation ('not ...'). It carries no case ending.
And not / but not — a connective 'and' joined to a negating particle that rules out or forbids what follows.
they (do) encompass / encircle masculine plural, indicative (-ūna)

A present-tense Form IV verb, third-person masculine plural ('they encompass'). The -ūna ending marks the masculine-plural doers in the indicative mood — a plain statement of fact ('they do encompass').
'they encompass / they comprehend fully' — to surround, enclose, or grasp something completely, so that nothing of it escapes one's reach or understanding.
with / by means of a thing / something
A preposition ('with/by') joined to the noun 'thing'. The '-in' ending marks the noun as indefinite ('a thing, something') and in the genitive case required after a preposition.
ش-ي-ء (sh-y-ʾ) — thing; to will, to want
'with something / with a thing' — by means of some thing or matter; 'shay'' is the most general word for 'a thing'.

A preposition. It is indeclinable and governs the following noun in the genitive case. Its primary force is point of origin ('starting from'), and it frequently expresses partitioning ('some of / part of').

A preposition whose core sense is 'from' — marking a starting point or origin (from a place, time, or source). It also commonly means 'of' or 'among' (part of a larger whole) and 'than' in comparisons.

A masculine verbal noun with the attached possessive pronoun -hi ('his'). The -i ending is genitive: this case typically follows a preposition or links the word to another as a possessor ('… of …'), and the pronoun appears here as -hi (rather than -hu) because it follows that -i vowel.
'His knowledge' — knowing, awareness, or learning, here possessed by Him. 'Ilm denotes sure, comprehensive knowledge as opposed to guesswork.

An exceptive particle (originally a fusion of 'in' + 'lā'). It is invariable — it has no case ending and does not change form. It introduces the item being excepted from, or restricted within, the preceding statement.

A particle of exception meaning 'except,' 'save,' or 'only.' It carves out something from a preceding general statement — most often a preceding negation — so that the excepted item alone is affirmed, giving the strong sense 'none … but' or 'nothing other than.'

The preposition bi ('in, with, by means of, concerning') attached to the relative/indefinite pronoun mā ('what, that which'). Both elements are invariable, so the phrase carries no case ending. A word governed by the preposition bi is understood to be in the genitive relationship.
A phrase meaning 'in/with that which' or 'in what' — combining the sense of 'by means of' or 'concerning' with an unspecified thing ('whatever it may be').
Past-tense (perfect) verb, third person masculine singular ('he'). The bare stem with no suffix is the basic 'he did' form. Its subject and object are typically supplied by context.
A past-tense verb 'he willed / he wished / he so desired'. It denotes an act of will or volition bringing something about.
A past-tense verb, third-person masculine singular ('he/it encompassed'). The final -a marks the completed action of a single masculine subject ('he/it did encompass / extend over').
و-س-ع (w-s-ʿ) — to be wide, spacious, to encompass
